And we begin this hour with the stories of two Syrian cities torn by conflict, Hama and Homs.
In a moment, a look back at Hama and a government-led massacre there in 1982 that crushed dissent and cast a long shadow over the country.
But, first, to Homs today, where tank shells crashed into residential neighborhoods.
It is the third largest city in Syria and a hub of recent anti-government protests.
NPR's Kelly McEvers traveled to the Lebanese-Syria border and met a man who recently fled from Homs.
Before the trouble started, Abu Omar was a house painter in Homs.
Syria's economy was opening up, people were building.
The kids had free education and health care.
Most people were focused on getting by, he says, not on political reform.
Nearly every neighbor had a relative who had been punished for trying to get involved in politics.
(Through translator) People disappear, nobody knows anything about them for 10, 12 years.
We used to think about it, but we couldn't do anything about it.
Then, Tunisia overthrew its dictator, then Egypt.
In Syria some teenagers in the southern city of Daraa sprayed graffiti on the walls of government buildings, calling for the doctor, a nickname for Syrian president Bashar Al-Assad, to go.
The teenagers were arrested and allegedly tortured and the protests spread.
Still, Abu Omar stayed home.
It wasn't until Syrian tanks and troops entered Daraa and other cities and hundreds of people started dying that Abu Omar decided he couldn't take it anymore.
Two-and-a-half weeks ago, he joined his first Friday protest.
(Speaking foreign language)
He realized that the oppression has become unbearable.
The entire Syrian people have to rise up or else there's going to be a genocide.
After last Friday's protests, tanks started rolling into Abu Omar's city, Homs.
Abu Omar watched from his balcony as tank shells destroyed a shopping center down the street.
(Speaking foreign language)
They sealed off all the entrances to the city, he says.
They cut off electricity, shot out water tanks, families ran out of bread.
Then, he says, security forces started going house to house.
(through translator) And instead of arresting the men, they decided to start arresting women under 50.
To any Arab man, the implication was clear: We will detain your women and rape them.
It was this that finally convinced Abu Omar to pack up the women and children and leave.
Now he and nearly 30 relatives are staying with family here in northern Lebanon.
Analysts in the region say it's people like Abu Omar who make up this second wave of a revolution.
First it's the activists who organize protests online and use satellite phones and Skype to get the word out.
Then, when the regime starts killing, detaining and torturing protesters, it's people like Abu Omar who get radicalized.
